speaker: interviewer
text: --2nd, 1996. Before we move on, I'd like to just figure out what happened to everyone in your family. You told me about your sister and her son. What happened to them? You left them in-- in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the baby, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and the baby and her mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her sister-in-law, which were all the family from her husband. He dropped them off near Plaszow, Cracow, by a gentile. Because he wanted to go to the camp and make connections to go to Hungary. He had some way of connections making. While he was there to makes-- make the connections, the guy, who they were there, went out and called in the SS. And they shoot him outside. All of them. And this is the-- this is-- after the war, I didn't know where it is. But when he came back, he was-- he was, by the way, he made it later to Hungary and Romania. And he was liberated in Romania. And after he came back, he digged them out and brought him to [INAUDIBLE]-- to [INAUDIBLE], was to-- to-- to-- because we was in Cracow. Which I was in Cracow three years ago, four years ago. I was in the [INAUDIBLE] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he buried--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- this is the stone. And-- and the names are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your-- your sister, Rifka--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifka Rosa, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and her son. What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents? As I showed you here, when I went to Bochnia, at that time with-- I told you, I ran out. I couldn't go into Bobov. And I came at night, to Bobov. And my parents didn't ask me to stay over shabbos. It was Thursday night. They didn't want-- so they couldn't convince me. And I says, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'll have to return myself. I-- I then supposed to stay in Bobov. Because I was sent out to Plaszow before. And I wasn't legal there. The only thing which saved me was that this Laski, the commandant, he told me, what are you doing here? I said, I didn't have the clothes so I am going now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I took the clothes with me, so I was allowed to go back to work. So at that time I-- he put to the-- to the railroad. Otherwise I would have been in the-- in-- in-- in-- at that time they made-- they made bomb-- Jewish clean. Because the young ones, there were-- there was no more young ones. So if I came in, it's bad luck. But I went-- he put me into the train, luckily. And he was-- he was a [NON-ENGLISH], this Laski the commandant. And he knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went-- came-- I came back to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --to what town was this here? Bochnia. So the Bobov rabbi's son lived there. So I was the first one to come in. And I told them about the story, what happened in Bobov. Because I-- I knew what's going on. At the house, [INAUDIBLE] was there. And what else are they asking about? What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents were staying in Bobov at that time. And I don't have to tell you the-- the railroad was-- was blocked. The township was blocked all around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, I heard when he wanted to go to hide, and he hid something there that-- well, what-- what do you call it, an [NON-ENGLISH]? The An-- an attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Attic. The-- the woman which cleaned called in and says, Jude. But anyway, they wouldn't have-- they wouldn't be saved. They wouldn't be saved anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I just want-- want to bring out with whom you have to-- with whom you got to deal. You know? And this was a-- a stupid woman. She lived from you. She-- I mean, that's what it is. And at that time, when it finished, I heard that they brought the whole transport [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to Gorlitz. Gorlitz is a-- is a town next to [INAUDIBLE]. Was-- was the commandant, the-- the-- the-- like, like, the governor. You know? From the [INAUDIBLE], the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Gorlitz was the second town. They took the whole transport and put them to-- to Gorlitz. They-- they call it in a-- in a factory, from what they make shoes, whatever there is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took myself. And I went to Gorlitz with the-- with the white-- this [INAUDIBLE]. And I came into judenrat. I want to take him out. So what they did, is they took me by-- by-- by this ear. And he says, I'll give you a kick. I'll kick in-- in the behind. And get out of here. Otherwise you're going to be dead too. You know? I don't know. I was-- it was-- it was stupidity for me. I didn't realize-- I thought maybe, maybe, I don't know, whatever. This-- this was the end of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have [INAUDIBLE], which you have here, this is the US side from Gorlitz. From-- from-- they used to send them to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's also think about the time that you were in Auschwitz, in the army, in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got there again in January, '43. What were the differences? From the army base that you remember, to Auschwitz, the death camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to army, to the-- to Auschwitz as a soldier, I was in barracks. And there was a-- one-- one division was for horses. I was-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. On Polish it's heavy machine [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what I mean? [SPEAKING POLISH]. This is in Polish. And I was by that. And as I told you before, when the-- when I went to the-- on the front there, I had-- I had the-- the-- the-- the base to carry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody carried the gun. And one carried two-- two boxes of-- of ammunition. So this was my-- this. But it was barracks. Barracks and then adjoining to the barracks was a-- a horse stable, for horses. Which, were also-- also for part of us. Which they were engaged in the horses. But this wasn't the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they keep the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't actually-- I wasn't in Auschwitz, in Auschwitz. I was brought to Auschwitz. And I was sent to block-- block 11. And I was in jail there. And in two weeks, three weeks, there was-- I was sent back. And I-- I didn't even see the camp too much. I saw more now, when I was there lately-- three years later, than before, that I saw there. So and-- and then they shipped me back to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't even think about the changes when you were there? It wasn't an issue in your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was change-- it wasn't the same. That wasn't the same. The-- those buildings weren't there. We didn't sleep in those buildings in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they-- they-- they restructured them or whatever. But this was-- Auschwitz was-- was a division of the 73rd [INAUDIBLE], which was in Katowice. Have you heard of Katowice? This was in Auschwitz. So I was in the second division. I was in Auschwitz. But my-- my main-- this was in Katowice. And the-- when the-- the-- when-- when I was there in the army, when they dragged us further, it got hot, a little bit, with the-- with-- with the Germans. They took us out for maneuvers. They took us to-- to a little town, Mykolaiv, near-- away from Auschwitz. And there we had to maneuvers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we had-- we digged the-- the the net. A wire net, whatever. You know? We wanted to hold back the-- their-- their-- their tanks with a wire net. It was stupid. You know what else? They didn't even have-- we-- we. They, I say we. We didn't even have a machine to knock in poles. But we-- we were dragging strings with with-- with-- with rocks, and to-- to knock-- to knock in, this here, the poles. You know? And this is true. This is what-- what-- the equipment we had against the Germans. Germans came in with tanks, with-- with-- with planes. You know? That's what it was. What this-- this was-- this was. But we were in Mykolaiv.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Ribnik. We went-- we went for-- for-- for-- for exercises.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to bring you back now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the-- to Birkenau, where most of your time was spent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you start building a resistance to what you were seeing? How-- how did it start with you? How did you move with what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our resistance to-- to-- to survive? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There is life in this nature. That's all I can tell you. I don't know. If you asked me how-- how? I don't know. Sometimes I think about myself, oh, I did the right thing? Because under-- under the Jewish law, maybe it's [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], you know what I mean? But this is how it was. Nobody did it. And I didn't. Did you ever hear about anyone surviving the gas chambers? Did it ever happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody survive-- let me tell you something. I heard, and I saw the movie. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the movie, now, from-- from him? 'Schindler's List?'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Schindler's List.' And in 'Schindler's List,' it's one part that he took out a-- a shipment from-- from Auschwitz. And everybody says he's a great man. You're asking me, I say, I don't believe it. I do not believe it. Finished. There was nobody. Nobody could get out from Auschwitz. Alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never heard about escapees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Escapees? Yeah, we had an escapee. We had an escapee. There was one escapee. He was-- was Daniel Oppsfeld. He was the same number than George. George. I don't remember the name. I-- I just-- but Daniel Oppsfeld, he was the same number from previous, and the same age than-- than the blockalteste. And he was also one of the main-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Four months, he was there. You know? And he was an old number. And he had a number in the 20s.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the end of it, he met-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had ideas. I don't know. Nobody-- nobody can read somebody else's mind. But-- but at the end, chose the beginning. He organized something with another guy, with two guys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to evade, to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? The other guy knew an SS man from-- from before, from old times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says he will take him out for money. I don't know. It-- it-- it-- it says that Daniel Oppsfeld had the money. And he was financing the money. And the other one was the one who bought the idea. And they went on the journey. It was three or four people on the journey. Next day, we march into the camp. He sits in a chair, Daniel. All the three, others too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back was our stick. You know? With the [INAUDIBLE] up. I made it. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS man took the money and shot him. And then they gave him vacation. So you ask about-- about coming to Auschwitz, and-- and letting-- let a-- a whole transport go because Schindler, well, I-- I don't know. I don't know. Well, I had to-- I have to believe it. Because he did it. He did an amazing job in the film. But I can't understand it. I can't understand that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there special transports that you recall, like the people from Theresienstadt that came? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was in '90-- in '43 came a family-- a family shipment from Theresienstadt. Families with kids. And they were-- and they were located-- maybe I was in D. They were maybe in F. Next-- next to us, what that's going in the [INAUDIBLE]. You know? So going to [INAUDIBLE] was also there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gypsies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, gypsies. Also-- also families. Next thing. They came in. And we heard that they treat them good. And they have-- they gave them kinder schools. And all-- all kind of food. And-- and they brought him in-- in whatever, the necessities. And this was going for months. And they were out. They encouraged them to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the end of it, it shows this with just a trick. A big trick to trick the people outside to say, how can you say that? You see this here. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the end of it, they liquidated them. There was-- there was a plan. And all of a sudden, at night, the trucks started rolling. And they emptied out till 1:00. And the same thing was with the-- with [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take the gypsies? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gypsies they took after that. After that. The gypsies, they cleaned out, '44, in the third quarter, '44. '44 in the third quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a whole camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after the Hungarians came the gypsies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they did a-- a whole camp of them at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole camp. You know, there were four crematoriums. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, four crematoriums, what-- what-- what they can produce? 10,000 a day. Any other transport that you remember, this en-masse transport or particular? Were the people that were brought that were shot rather than went through the-- through the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the shooting case. The shooting case. I told you about the Americans. They came from Hamburg. And this woman, she played a star. You know, dancing. And the SS men were very busy with her. And meanwhile, she go-- went around. She danced around to him and grabbed the gun and-- and killed him. And then it started. They-- they massacred the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Schillinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other cases as well, were there non-Jewish groups that ended up in the gas chambers and then in the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Singles. Not meaningful. Not meaningful. Wasn't meant to-- to-- to just eliminate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one-- one sold meat. Another one, this here, you know what I mean? Or a mayor or that, and here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or that's what it was. But-- but mass-- mass shipments, Jewish. I tell you the truth. I didn't even know that they killed Pollacks. I didn't see it. I didn't see it. Except [INAUDIBLE] and the two [NON-ENGLISH], who did [INAUDIBLE], you know, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], they thought they are safe. Because they weren't in our block. They weren't with us. They were in number two, in Auschwitz. Where their leader is, where the SS men, [INAUDIBLE], whatever, whoever he was, Hoss was in-- in Auschwitz. He put them in. You know, he privileged him. So he made him kapo. But by the end of it, when the crematorium in Auschwitz was meaningless, they put him in back to crematorium here, under Kaminsky. And he-- he kept his mouth shut. There he had no words. Because there was only Jews. And Kaminsky was more than him. But-- but he-- he made-- he killed Kaminsky. He had still-- his Polish head worked this way, that he should-- they should eliminate him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you can-- when you think about it, you think very few Polish people were killed, Polish Catholic people. Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught-- they killed this [INAUDIBLE]. The-- the kapo, which he saved me. He saved me. But he's cautious. I tell you, he wrote down the-- the SS-- SS Sturmer, not Sturmfuhrer. Sturm-- Sturmmann. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were individual cases?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were individual. There-- there was no killing of Poles. There were-- maybe in intelligentsia sometimes. If they-- if they smelled a revolt. So they cleaned up. You know what I mean? The high echelon, but not the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as you knew, when the children came into the camp, they were immediately sent to the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place for-- for kids. There was no kids. No kids. There were plenty-- plenty times which we saw with our eyes, [INAUDIBLE]-- and the other SS fuhrers, you know, they brought in their-- their-- their-- their friends, girlfriends, ladies, whatever this is. SS, also. And they-- if they want to make a show [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they-- they killed before they went in. The SS just turned around and gave them a bullet in the back. This was fun. And-- and-- and this was to show off what he can do. And this was the guy, [INAUDIBLE]. And another guy was there, a scharfuhrer also, who were-- who were responsible for the massacre from the-- from the few hundred people which were before my commander, which-- which which I heard before New Year's they-- they cleaned up the whole thing. Because they said they [INAUDIBLE]. Or they-- they think they-- they organize something, and they clean them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the people from 1942, just before you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. Before I got there, and before they-- I-- and then they-- they brought in the others under commander, which they came from-- from Bialystok, from Lomza, from Grodno. And from-- from [INAUDIBLE] there, from near Warsaw there, and then another town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you mainly Polish, in the Sonderkommando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mainly Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mainly Polish? No. No way. No way. Little Polish. Well, Polish, we were from-- if you-- from Bialystok, and from Lomza, This was Polish. But there were French. There were Holland. And there were Slavs, Slavics. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews. And one-- one Frenchman, Maurice, he came from Algiers. From Algiers. He must have been in Paris. And they sent him-- when the Warsaw ghetto was-- the uprising, they sent him to Warsaw. W Because he didn't speak Polish, and not Jewish, but French. And most probably they brought him there to hang, or something like that. That's what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's move on. Any other thoughts about the time in Auschwitz that you would like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, in the camp? Nothing I can think of now.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll move on. And if there's something, you'll tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when I came back-- when I was already liberated, so when the Russians came in, I went to my apartment, where we lived. And we lived in a good apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this apartment was taken by a Volksdeutsche. And there was a bakery. We lived in a three, four-story building, on the third floor. Nice apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the bottom was a bakery. It was [INAUDIBLE], a-- a Jewish. So [INAUDIBLE], he was-- he was a Volksdeutsche. He took over the bakery. And he took over-- he took over our apartment. Because this was one of the nicest apartments. We had three bedrooms, a big apartment, beautiful, with a balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came back, I went to this apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came in. I went in the apartment. I picked the door. I went in the apartment. All of a sudden, this little guy, 10 year, came up. Home, nobody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him by-- by-- by the neck. And I threw him out the stairs. And he didn't show up no more. But then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had my friend, this Schlomo Dragon. He went-- he was with me. We-- and we-- we took this apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And whoever came had a foot in my apartment. But then when was a time-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --when I went with the Russians to Auschwitz. I went there. I spent, there, more than a week. Maybe 10 days. And I showed them places, explaining to them from place to place, where the-- this here, what was everything. And there was this Captain, Levi, Levi, a Jew. A Jew. And then there was a mayor. I don't know, he was a Jew or not, but a fine man. And they treat me as one of theirs, with-- with food and with sleeping, and this. And they gave me-- by the end of it, when I show them, they gave me a notebook. And he says, you know, now write. And I gave it to him in writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? My brother, the older brother, which is now in Belgium, he was in Russia. He saw me in Russia, in 1945, on the newsreel. And my name was Henryk Tauber. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took pictures out of us. And they showed a newsreel there. And here they didn't-- he-- we didn't see this here at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I went home they gave me a letter. A letter with a protection letter, with you know, it had-- had [INAUDIBLE]. When I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guess what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't go into the house. I had a bicycle there. And I had the-- I-- I-- I made a-- a trip to Auschwitz, without a wheelbarrow. Because I didn't have anything to-- to-- to go. And I pushed a wheelbarrow from Auschwitz with-- with-- with blankets in order to have something to start with. And I left it there, the bicycle and the blankets. When I came back, I couldn't go into the apartment. Because the Polish, they laid a hand of it. I didn't have the right to take the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took my letter. And at that time, it wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. It was a city. It was an [INAUDIBLE] city. You know what I mean? It wasn't just a-- a-- and I went to the commandant [GERMAN]. You know what it means, commandant [GERMAN]? The commandant-- the-- the war commandant, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him the story. And I showed him the letter. He called up-- [LAUGHS] The-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] there. They-- they were-- they were the police. They gave me-- they gave me my apartment back so fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't even turn back. You know? But I wanted to show with whom I was dealing. And at that time, whoever came to [INAUDIBLE] at the time, from the [INAUDIBLE], in the beginning, came to my apartment and to this house. How many people came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came back from-- from the camps. You know, in the beginning, in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Germans gave orders to the judenrat. We need 500 people. The order-- the judenrat was-- [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And one was a-- [INAUDIBLE] was-- was like-- like, the head of the police, the OD. And they gave them the orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had to bring order-- bring back the orders. You know what I mean? They make-- make-- what I make-- if-- if they gave-- gave-- gave him an amount, I need 400 to-- to-- to-- to-- to the camps, 500. So they supplied. They didn't go for it. But you know, they-- they [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And those ladies, girls, a lot of girls, went to work in Oberschlesian. In Schlesian. Schlesian, near Breslau, near Szczecin, near-- near-- the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE]. You know what I mean, [INAUDIBLE]. After the war, a lot of them come back. After the war a lot of them came back. This wasn't-- some of them was concentration camps. They didn't survive. And some of them was working camps. And-- and some of them survived. Some of them survived. So there was a few back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many men. Not many men.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother-in-law was-- was-- well, didn't come back. My brother-in-law didn't come back. He couldn't-- he couldn't survive. Some of them was-- was-- were-- were terrible camps, concentration camps. And some of them was-- was just-- I mean, farmers working in factories, working. And where-- where they had-- where the SS didn't have the direct hand on it, or the political, the-- the gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in your home town, in [? Janow? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long? After the war, how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I came home. It was before Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early September.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what-- what did I say? Rosh Hashanah? Actually, no. Rosh Hashanah was-- was this-- the beginning of the war. It was the beginning of the war. To [? Janow ?] I came in January the 19th, I told you. January the 21, two days, took me, to come back. I thought the beginning of the war was Rosh Hashanah, when I came back home from-- from the army. I came back for Rosh Hashanah. And my parents were in Cracow at that time. But after the war, I was already, second day in [? Janow. ?] I told you the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right. We will continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.
